syllabus.title: Functions and graphs
syllabus.content: Represent, interpret, and analyse functions, including domain, range, transformations, intersections, and graphical features.

syllabus.title: Graphing techniques
syllabus.content: Use transformations and graphing techniques to analyse, sketch, and interpret functions.

syllabus.title: Logarithms and exponentials
syllabus.content: Use logarithmic and exponential functions, equations, and transformations in algebraic and applied contexts.

syllabus.title: Differential calculus
syllabus.content: Use derivatives to determine rates of change, gradients, stationary points, tangents, normals, and optimisation results.

syllabus.title: Applications of differentiation
syllabus.content: Apply differentiation to rates of change, curve features, optimisation, and contextual modelling problems.

syllabus.title: Integral calculus
syllabus.content: Use definite and indefinite integrals, areas under curves, and accumulation ideas to solve mathematical and applied problems.

syllabus.title: Integral calculus
syllabus.content: Use integration methods, definite integrals, areas, and accumulation to solve mathematical and applied problems.

syllabus.title: Modelling financial situations
syllabus.content: Use sequences, series, interest factors, annuities, loans, and recurrence relationships to model financial situations.

syllabus.title: Trigonometric functions
syllabus.content: Work with trigonometric functions, identities, exact values, equations, graphs, and practical angle relationships.

syllabus.title: Trigonometric functions and identities
syllabus.content: Use trigonometric identities and algebraic manipulation to prove identities and solve trigonometric problems.

syllabus.title: Trigonometric functions and graphs
syllabus.content: Analyse trigonometric functions, graphs, equations, periodic behaviour, and transformations.

syllabus.title: Probability and discrete probability distributions
syllabus.content: Use probability rules, conditional probability, discrete distributions, and contextual probability models.

syllabus.title: Descriptive statistics and bivariate data analysis
syllabus.content: Use scatterplots, association, least-squares regression lines, interpolation, extrapolation, and contextual interpretation to analyse bivariate datasets.

syllabus.title: Random variables
syllabus.content: Use discrete and continuous random variables, probability density functions, normal models, percentiles, and expected-value reasoning.

questions.title: Pearson correlation from a scatterplot
questions.subtopic: Descriptive statistics and bivariate data analysis
questions.promptLatex: The number of bees leaving a hive was observed and recorded over \(14\) days at different times of the day. Which Pearson's correlation coefficient best describes the observations? Options: A. \(-0.8\), B. \(-0.2\), C. \(0.2\), D. \(0.8\).
questions.answerLatex: D. The scatterplot shows a strong positive linear association, best described by \(r\approx0.8\).
workingLatex: The points trend upward as time increases, so the correlation is positive and relatively strong.

assets.label: 2023 Q1 bees scatterplot
assets.alt: Scatterplot showing number of bees leaving a hive at different times of day, with a strong positive association.

questions.title: Die and spinner score probability
questions.promptLatex: A game involves throwing a die and spinning a spinner labelled \(1,2,3,4\). The sum of the two numbers obtained is the score. What is the probability of getting a score of \(7\) or more? Options: A. \(\frac16\), B. \(\frac14\), C. \(\frac{5}{18}\), D. \(\frac{5}{12}\).
questions.answerLatex: D. There are \(6\times4=24\) equally likely outcomes. Scores of at least \(7\) occur in \(10\) outcomes, so the probability is \(\frac{10}{24}=\frac{5}{12}\).
workingLatex: For spinner values \(1,2,3,4\), the die values giving a score of at least \(7\) are \(6\), \(5,6\), \(4,5,6\), and \(3,4,5,6\).

assets.label: 2023 Q2 die and spinner
assets.alt: A die and a four-section spinner labelled 1, 2, 3 and 4.

assets.label: 2023 Q2 partially completed score table
assets.alt: Partially completed table of scores for die values 1 to 6 and spinner values 1 to 4.

questions.title: Domain of a rational radical function
questions.promptLatex: What is the domain of \(f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x}}\)? Options: A. \(x<1\), B. \(x\le1\), C. \(x>1\), D. \(x\ge1\).
questions.answerLatex: A. The denominator \(\sqrt{1-x}\) must be real and non-zero, so \(1-x>0\), giving \(x<1\).
workingLatex: Require \(1-x>0\) because the square root is in the denominator.

questions.title: Polynomial graph recognition
questions.promptLatex: The graph of a polynomial is shown. Which row of the table is correct for this polynomial? Options: A. \(y=-(x-b)(x-c)^2\), \(b>0\), \(c<0\); B. \(y=-(x-b)(x-c)^2\), \(b<0\), \(c>0\); C. \(y=-x(x-b)(x-c)\), \(b>0\), \(c<0\); D. \(y=-x(x-b)(x-c)\), \(b<0\), \(c>0\).
questions.answerLatex: B. The graph crosses the \(x\)-axis at a negative value and touches the \(x\)-axis at a positive value, with negative leading behaviour. This matches \(y=-(x-b)(x-c)^2\), \(b<0\), \(c>0\).
workingLatex: A simple root appears to the left of the origin, so \(b<0\).
A repeated root appears to the right of the origin, so \(c>0\).
The right-hand end goes down, consistent with the negative leading coefficient.

assets.label: 2023 Q4 polynomial graph
assets.alt: Polynomial graph crossing the x-axis left of the origin and touching the x-axis right of the origin before turning downward.

questions.title: Odd function integral and shaded area
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows the graph \(y=f(x)\), where \(f(x)\) is an odd function. The shaded area is \(1\) square unit. The number \(a\), where \(a>1\), is chosen so that \(\int_0^a f(x)\,dx=0\). What is the value of \(\int_{-a}^{1}f(x)\,dx\)? Options: A. \(-1\), B. \(0\), C. \(1\), D. \(3\).
questions.answerLatex: A. Since \(f\) is odd and \(\int_0^a f(x)\,dx=0\), \(\int_{-a}^{0}f(x)\,dx=0\). The shaded region from \(0\) to \(1\) lies below the \(x\)-axis and has area \(1\), so \(\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx=-1\). Therefore \(\int_{-a}^{1}f(x)\,dx=-1\).
workingLatex: \(\int_0^1f(x)\,dx=-1\) from the shaded area below the axis.

assets.label: 2023 Q5 odd function shaded area
assets.alt: Graph of an odd function with a shaded region below the x-axis from 0 to 1 and labels negative a and a.

questions.title: Sketch from derivative sign table
questions.promptLatex: The table gives the signs of the first and second derivatives of a function \(y=f(x)\) for different values of \(x\): \[\begin{array}{c|ccc}x&-2&0&2\\\hline f'(x)&+&0&+\\ f''(x)&-&0&+\end{array}\] Which of the given options is a possible sketch of \(y=f(x)\)?
questions.answerLatex: C. The function is increasing on both sides of \(x=0\), has \(f'(0)=0\), and changes concavity from down to up, so the sketch should show a stationary point of inflection at \(x=0\).
workingLatex: \(f'(x)>0\) at \(x=-2\) and \(x=2\), so the function is increasing on both sides.
\(f'(0)=0\) but the derivative sign does not change, suggesting a stationary point of inflection.
\(f''(x)\) changes from negative to positive, matching concavity down then concavity up.

assets.label: 2023 Q6 derivative sign table and graph options
assets.alt: Derivative sign table and four possible sketches for y equals f of x.

questions.title: Chain rule for a composite function
questions.answerLatex: A. By the chain rule, \(y'=f'(g(x))g'(x)\). At \(x=5\), this is \(f'(1)\times2=-4\times2=-8\).

questions.title: Solving a logarithmic equation
questions.promptLatex: What is the solution of the equation \(\log_a x^3=b\), where \(a\) and \(b\) are positive constants? Options: A. \(x=b^{a/3}\), B. \(x=a^{b/3}\), C. \(x=\frac{ba}{3}\), D. \(x=\frac{a^b}{3}\).

questions.title: Even function from an arbitrary function
questions.promptLatex: Let \(f(x)\) be any function with domain all real numbers. Which of the following is an even function, regardless of the choice of \(f(x)\)? Options: A. \(2f(x)\), B. \(f(f(x))\), C. \((f(-x))^2\), D. \(f(x)f(-x)\).
questions.answerLatex: D. If \(h(x)=f(x)f(-x)\), then \(h(-x)=f(-x)f(x)=h(x)\), so \(h\) is even for any function \(f\).

questions.title: Parabola intersection length under scaling
questions.promptLatex: The graph \(y=x^2\) meets the line \(y=k\), where \(k>0\), at points \(P\) and \(Q\). The length of the interval \(PQ\) is \(L\). Let \(a\) be a positive number. The graph \(y=\frac{x^2}{a^2}\) meets the line \(y=k\) at points \(S\) and \(T\). What is the length of \(ST\)? Options: A. \(\frac{L}{a}\), B. \(\frac{L}{a^2}\), C. \(aL\), D. \(a^2L\).
questions.answerLatex: C. For \(y=x^2\), the intersections with \(y=k\) are \(x=\pm\sqrt{k}\), so \(L=2\sqrt{k}\). For \(y=\frac{x^2}{a^2}\), \(x=\pm a\sqrt{k}\), so \(ST=2a\sqrt{k}=aL\).

assets.label: 2023 Q10 parabola and horizontal line
assets.alt: Graph of y equals x squared meeting horizontal line y equals k at points P and Q.

questions.title: Discrete probability table
questions.promptLatex: The probability distribution table for a discrete random variable \(X\) is shown: \[\begin{array}{c|c}x&P(X=x)\\\hline 1&0.4\\2&0.2\\3&\ ?\end{array}\] What is the value of \(P(X=3)\)? Options: A. \(0.2\), B. \(0.4\), C. \(1.2\), D. \(2.0\).
questions.answerLatex: B. The probabilities must add to \(1\), so \(P(X=3)=1-0.4-0.2=0.4\).

questions.title: Recognising an exponential graph
questions.promptLatex: Which graph could represent \(y=4^x\)?
questions.answerLatex: A. The function \(y=4^x\) is positive, increasing, concave up, and has horizontal asymptote \(y=0\).
workingLatex: The graph is always positive and approaches \(0\) as \(x\to-\infty\).

assets.label: 2025 Q2 exponential graph options
assets.alt: Four multiple-choice graph options for y equals 4 to the power x.

questions.title: Domain of a radical function
questions.promptLatex: What is the domain of the function \(y=\sqrt{6-x^2}\)? Options: A. \((0,6)\), B. \([0,6]\), C. \((-\sqrt6,\sqrt6)\), D. \([-\sqrt6,\sqrt6]\).
questions.answerLatex: D. The expression under the square root must be non-negative: \(6-x^2\ge0\), so \(-\sqrt6\le x\le\sqrt6\).

questions.title: Cubic graph recognition
questions.promptLatex: Which graph best represents \(y=-5x(x-2)(3-x)\)?
questions.answerLatex: C. Rewriting gives \(y=5x(x-2)(x-3)\), a positive-leading-coefficient cubic with roots \(0\), \(2\), and \(3\).
workingLatex: The graph has x-intercepts at \(0\), \(2\), and \(3\), with negative left-end and positive right-end behaviour.

questions.title: Indefinite integral of a radical expression
questions.promptLatex: What is \(\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{x+5}}\,dx\)? Options: A. \(\frac12\sqrt{x+5}+C\), B. \(2\sqrt{x+5}+C\), C. \(-\frac12\sqrt{x+5}+C\), D. \(-2\sqrt{x+5}+C\).
questions.answerLatex: B. Since \(\int (x+5)^{-1/2}\,dx=2(x+5)^{1/2}+C\), the answer is \(2\sqrt{x+5}+C\).

questions.title: Function transformation graph
questions.promptLatex: The graph of \(y=f(x)\) is shown. Which option is the graph of \(y=-f(-x)\)?
questions.answerLatex: C. The transformation \(f(-x)\) reflects the graph in the \(y\)-axis, and the negative sign reflects it in the \(x\)-axis. The transformed graph has vertical asymptote \(x=2\), horizontal asymptote \(y=-1\), and y-intercept \(-3\).
workingLatex: Then reflect in the \(x\)-axis to form \(y=-f(-x)\).
The resulting asymptotes and intercept match option C.

questions.title: Relative frequency for a weighted die
questions.promptLatex: A ten-sided die has faces numbered 1 to 10. The probability of obtaining 1 is greater than the probability of obtaining any other number, and the numbers 2 to 10 are equally likely. When the die is rolled 153 times, a 1 is obtained 72 times. Using the relative frequency of rolling a 1, which is the best estimate for the probability of rolling a 10? Options: A. \(\frac1{17}\), B. \(\frac1{11}\), C. \(\frac1{10}\), D. \(\frac19\).
questions.answerLatex: A. The estimated probability of rolling 1 is \(\frac{72}{153}=\frac8{17}\), so the remaining probability is \(\frac9{17}\). This is shared equally by the nine other faces, giving \(\frac1{17}\).
workingLatex: Each of the nine remaining faces has probability \(\frac{9/17}{9}=\frac1{17}\).

questions.title: Normal distribution above zero
questions.promptLatex: The minimum daily temperature of a town each year follows a normal distribution with its mean equal to its standard deviation. The minimum daily temperature was recorded over one year. What percentage of the recorded minimum daily temperatures was above zero degrees? Options: A. \(16\%\), B. \(50\%\), C. \(68\%\), D. \(84\%\).
questions.answerLatex: D. If \(\mu=\sigma\), then \(z=\frac{0-\mu}{\sigma}=-1\). Therefore \(P(X>0)=P(Z>-1)\approx84\%\).
workingLatex: Since \(\mu=\sigma\), zero is one standard deviation below the mean.
For a normal distribution, about \(84\%\) of values lie above \(z=-1\).

questions.title: Estimate from a derivative graph
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows the graph of \(y=f'(x)\). Given \(f(1)=6\), which interval includes the best estimate for \(f(1.1)\)? Options: A. \([6.2,6.4)\), B. \([6.0,6.2)\), C. \([5.8,6.0)\), D. \([5.6,5.8)\).
questions.answerLatex: B. From the graph, \(f'(1)\) is about \(2\), so \(f(1.1)\approx f(1)+0.1f'(1)\approx6+0.2=6.2\). The graph decreases over this small interval, so the estimate lies just below \(6.2\), in \([6.0,6.2)\).
workingLatex: Use the local linear approximation \(f(1.1)\approx f(1)+(0.1)f'(1)\).
Since \(f'\) is decreasing near \(x=1\), the accumulated change is slightly less than \(0.2\).

questions.title: Stationary points of a composite function
questions.promptLatex: The graph of \(y=f(x)\), with all its stationary points, is shown. How many stationary points does the graph of \(y=f(e^x)\) have? Options: A. \(0\), B. \(1\), C. \(2\), D. \(3\).
questions.answerLatex: C. For \(g(x)=f(e^x)\), \(g'(x)=e^x f'(e^x)\). Since \(e^x>0\), stationary points occur when \(f'(e^x)=0\). The input \(e^x\) is always positive, so only the stationary points of \(f\) with positive input are counted. The graph shows two such stationary points.
workingLatex: The graph of \(f\) has two stationary points for positive input values.

questions.title: Quadratic turning point from a graph
questions.promptLatex: The graph of the quadratic function \(h=t^2-8t+12\) is shown. (a) Find the values of \(t\) and \(h\) at the turning point of the graph. (b) The graph shows \(h=12\) when \(t=0\). What is the other value of \(t\) for which \(h=12\)?
questions.answerLatex: Completing the square gives \(h=(t-4)^2-4\), so the turning point is \((4,-4)\). By symmetry, the other value of \(t\) where \(h=12\) is \(8\).
workingLatex: \(h=t^2-8t+12=(t-4)^2-4\), so at the turning point \(t=4\) and \(h=-4\).
The axis of symmetry is \(t=4\). Since \(t=0\) gives \(h=12\), the matching point on the other side is \(t=8\).

questions.title: Bivariate data and least-squares regression
questions.promptLatex: A research study compared each participant's average daily television watching time, \(x\) minutes, with their average daily exercise time, \(y\) minutes, over a three-month period. The data are shown in the scatterplot. (a) State the form and direction of the bivariate dataset. (b) The least-squares regression line is \(y=64.3-0.7x\). Interpret the slope and the \(y\)-intercept in context. (c) Use the line to estimate the average daily exercise time for \(x=42\). (d) Explain why the line should not be used to estimate the exercise time for a person who watches television for two hours per day.
questions.answerLatex: The association is linear and negative. The slope means that, for each additional minute of average daily television watching, the predicted average daily exercise time decreases by \(0.7\) minutes. The \(y\)-intercept means that a participant who watches no television is predicted to exercise for \(64.3\) minutes per day. For \(x=42\), \(y=64.3-0.7(42)=34.9\), so the estimate is \(34.9\) minutes. Two hours is outside the range of the observed data, so this would be unreliable extrapolation.
workingLatex: Slope: \(-0.7\), so predicted exercise time falls by \(0.7\) minutes per extra minute of television watching.
The value \(x=120\) is outside the plotted data range, so the prediction would be an extrapolation.

questions.promptLatex: A sound wave can be modelled using \(P(t)=k\sin(at)\), where \(P\) is air pressure in Pascals, \(t\) is time in milliseconds, and \(k\) and \(a\) are constants. (a) Write the equation for \(P_1(t)\), a sound wave with amplitude \(2\) Pascals and period \(5\) ms. (b) The graph of \(P_1(t)\) is shown. On the diagram, sketch \(P_2(t)=4\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{10}\right)\) for \(0\le t\le10\). questions.title: Stationary points of an exponential quotient
questions.promptLatex: Consider the function \(f(x)=\frac{x^2}{e^x}\). (a) Find the stationary points of the function and determine their nature. (b) A partially completed graph of \(f(x)=\frac{x^2}{e^x}\) is shown. Use your answer from part (a) to complete the graph.
questions.answerLatex: Writing \(f(x)=x^2e^{-x}\), \(f'(x)=2xe^{-x}-x^2e^{-x}=\frac{x(2-x)}{e^x}\). Hence stationary points occur at \(x=0\) and \(x=2\). Since \(f'(x)\) changes from negative to positive at \(0\), \((0,0)\) is a minimum. Since \(f'(x)\) changes from positive to negative at \(2\), \(\left(2,\frac4{e^2}\right)\) is a maximum.
workingLatex: The sign of \(f'(x)\) is negative, then positive, then negative across \(x=0\) and \(x=2\), so there is a minimum at \((0,0)\) and a maximum at \(\left(2,\frac4{e^2}\right)\).

questions.title: Equation of a tangent
questions.promptLatex: Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=5x^3-\frac{2}{x^2}-9\) at the point \((1,-6)\).
questions.answerLatex: The derivative is \(y'=15x^2+4x^{-3}\), so the gradient at \(x=1\) is \(19\). The tangent through \((1,-6)\) is \(y+6=19(x-1)\), hence \(y=19x-25\).

questions.title: Matching a future value with an annuity
questions.promptLatex: The table shows future value interest factors for an annuity of \($1\): \[\begin{array}{c|cccccc} n\backslash r & 0.005 & 0.01 & 0.015 & 0.02 & 0.03 & 0.06 \\ 7 & 7.10588 & 7.21354 & 7.32300 & 7.43428 & 7.66246 & 8.39384 \\ 28 & 29.97452 & 32.12910 & 34.48148 & 37.05121 & 42.93092 & 68.52811 \\ 56 & 64.44140 & 74.58098 & 86.79754 & 101.55826 & 141.15377 & 418.82235 \\ 84 & 104.07393 & 130.67227 & 166.17264 & 213.86661 & 365.88054 & 2209.41674\end{array}\] Lin invests \($21\,000\) for 7 years at \(6\%\) per annum, compounding monthly. Yemi wants the same future value by depositing a fixed amount at the end of each month for 7 years in an account with the same interest rate. Determine Yemi's monthly deposit.
questions.answerLatex: There are \(84\) monthly periods and the monthly rate is \(0.005\). Lin's future value is \(21000(1.005)^{84}\approx31927.76\). Using the annuity factor \(104.07393\), Yemi's monthly deposit is \(31927.76\div104.07393\approx\$306.78\).

questions.title: Trigonometric identity proof
questions.promptLatex: Prove that \(\frac{\sin^4\theta+\cos^4\theta+2\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}{\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}=\sec^2\theta\,\cosec^2\theta\).
questions.answerLatex: The numerator is \((\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta)^2=1\). Hence the left side is \(\frac{1}{\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}=\sec^2\theta\,\cosec^2\theta\), as required.

questions.title: Translated parabola parameter
questions.promptLatex: The parabola with equation \(y=(x-1)(x-5)\) is translated both horizontally to the right and vertically up by \(k\) units, where \(k\) is positive. The translated parabola passes through \((6,11)\). Find \(k\).
questions.answerLatex: After translating right and up by \(k\), substitute \(x-k\) for \(x\) and \(y-k\) for \(y\): \(11-k=(6-k-1)(6-k-5)\). This gives \(k^2-5k-6=0\), so \(k=6\) because \(k\) is positive.

questions.title: Reducing-balance loan model
questions.promptLatex: A borrower obtains a reducing-balance loan of \($800\,000\). Interest is charged at \(0.5\%\) monthly, compounded monthly. On the last day of each month, interest is added and then a repayment of \($5740\) is made. Let \(A_n\) be the balance owing at the end of \(n\) months. (a) Show that \(A_2=800000(1.005)^2-5740(1.005)-5740\). (b) Show that \(A_n=1148000-348000(1.005)^n\). (c) Find when the balance owing is first less than \($400\,000\).
questions.answerLatex: The recurrence is \(A_n=1.005A_{n-1}-5740\). This gives \(A_2=800000(1.005)^2-5740(1.005)-5740\). Summing the geometric repayment terms gives \(A_n=800000(1.005)^n-5740\frac{(1.005)^n-1}{0.005}=1148000-348000(1.005)^n\). Solving \(1148000-348000(1.005)^n<400000\) gives \(n>153.4\ldots\), so the balance is first less than \($400\,000\) after \(154\) months.

questions.title: Conditional probability from selection odds
questions.promptLatex: Amara, Bala and Cassie have nominated for a soccer team, and exactly one will be selected. Their chances of selection are in the ratio \(1:2:3\), respectively. The probability that the team wins is \(0.5\) if Amara is selected, \(0.4\) if Bala is selected, and \(0.2\) if Cassie is selected. Given that the team wins, find the probability that Amara was selected.
questions.answerLatex: The selection probabilities are \(\frac16\), \(\frac13\), and \(\frac12\). Therefore \[P(\text{Amara}\mid W)=\frac{\frac16\times0.5}{\frac16\times0.5+\frac13\times0.4+\frac12\times0.2}=\frac{5}{19}.\]

questions.title: Continuous random variable percentile
questions.promptLatex: A continuous random variable \(X\) has probability density function \[f(x)=\begin{cases}0,&x<1,\\ \frac1x,&1\le x\le e,\\ 0,&x>e.\end{cases}\] (a) Find the mode and justify your answer. (b) Calculate the value of the 25th percentile, \(Q_1\), correct to three decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: On \([1,e]\), \(f(x)=\frac1x\) is decreasing, so the mode is \(1\). For the 25th percentile, \(\int_1^{Q_1}\frac1x\,dx=0.25\), so \(\ln Q_1=0.25\) and \(Q_1=e^{0.25}\approx1.284\).
workingLatex: \(f(x)=\frac1x\) is largest at the left endpoint of \([1,e]\), so the mode is \(1\).

questions.title: Normal model for sheep weights
questions.promptLatex: (a) In a flock of \(12\,600\) sheep, the ratio of males to females is \(1:20\). The weights of the male sheep are normally distributed with mean \(76.2\) kg and standard deviation \(6.8\) kg. In the flock, \(15\) male sheep each weigh more than \(x\) kg. Find \(x\). (b) The female sheep weights are also normally distributed but have a smaller mean and smaller standard deviation than the male sheep weights. Explain whether it could be expected that \(300\) females each weigh more than \(x\) kg.
questions.answerLatex: There are \(12600\div21=600\) male sheep, and \(15\) out of \(600\) is \(2.5\%\). This corresponds to approximately two standard deviations above the mean, so \(x=76.2+2(6.8)=89.8\) kg. For females, the smaller mean and smaller standard deviation make the corresponding \(z\)-score larger than \(2\), so fewer than \(2.5\%\) should exceed \(x\); it would not be expected that \(300\) females exceed \(x\).
workingLatex: \(15\div600=0.025=2.5\%\), so \(x\) is about \(2\) standard deviations above the mean.
For females, \(z=\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\) is larger because both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\) are smaller, so the upper-tail percentage is less than \(2.5\%\).

questions.title: Common tangent to a logarithmic graph and parabola
questions.promptLatex: The graphs of \(y=e\ln x\) and \(y=ax^2+c\) are shown. The line \(y=x\) is a tangent to both graphs at their point of intersection. Find the values of \(a\) and \(c\).
questions.answerLatex: The common tangent has gradient \(1\). Since \(\frac{d}{dx}(e\ln x)=\frac{e}{x}\), setting \(\frac{e}{x}=1\) gives \(x=e\), and then \(y=e\). For \(y=ax^2+c\), the derivative is \(2ax\), so \(2ae=1\) and \(a=\frac{1}{2e}\). Substituting \((e,e)\) gives \(e=ae^2+c=\frac{e}{2}+c\), hence \(c=\frac{e}{2}\).
workingLatex: At the point of tangency, both graphs have gradient \(1\).
The point of intersection is therefore \((e,e)\).

questions.title: Areas under x sin x using a series
questions.promptLatex: (a) Show that \(\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x-x\cos x)=x\sin x\). (b) Hence, find \(\int_0^{2025\pi}x\sin x\,dx\). (c) The regions bounded by the \(x\)-axis and the graph of \(y=x\sin x\), for \(x\ge0\), are shown. Let \(A_n=\int_{(n-1)\pi}^{n\pi}x\sin x\,dx\), where \(n\) is a positive integer. It can be shown that \(|A_n|=(2n-1)\pi\). Find the exact total area of the regions bounded by \(y=x\sin x\) and the \(x\)-axis between \(x=0\) and \(x=2025\pi\).
questions.answerLatex: Using the product rule, \(\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x-x\cos x)=\cos x-(\cos x-x\sin x)=x\sin x\). Hence \(\int_0^{2025\pi}x\sin x\,dx=[\sin x-x\cos x]_0^{2025\pi}=2025\pi\). For total area, sum \(\pi+3\pi+5\pi+\cdots+4049\pi\), an arithmetic series with \(2025\) terms. Therefore the exact total area is \(2025^2\pi\) square units.
workingLatex: \(\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x-x\cos x)=\cos x-(\cos x-x\sin x)=x\sin x\).
The sum of the first \(2025\) odd numbers is \(2025^2\), so the total area is \(2025^2\pi\).

questions.title: Wire area optimisation
questions.promptLatex: A piece of wire is \(100\) cm long. Some is used to make a circle of radius \(r\) cm, and the remainder is used to make an equilateral triangle of side length \(x\) cm. (a) Show that the combined area is \[A(x)=\frac{\sqrt3}{4}x^2+\frac{(100-3x)^2}{4\pi}.\] (b) By considering this quadratic function, show that the maximum value of \(A(x)\) occurs when all the wire is used for the circle.
questions.answerLatex: Since \(2\pi r+3x=100\), \(r=\frac{100-3x}{2\pi}\). Hence \(A(x)=\pi r^2+\frac{\sqrt3}{4}x^2=\frac{(100-3x)^2}{4\pi}+\frac{\sqrt3}{4}x^2\). This quadratic has positive leading coefficient, so its maximum on the physical domain occurs at an endpoint. At \(x=0\), the area is \(\frac{2500}{\pi}\approx796\). At \(x=\frac{100}{3}\), the triangle-only area is about \(481\). Therefore the maximum occurs when all wire is used for the circle.
workingLatex: The coefficient of \(x^2\) is positive, so the quadratic has a minimum turning point and the maximum on the domain is at an endpoint.

questions.title: Exponential area and an inequality for e
questions.promptLatex: The shaded region is bounded by the graph \(y=\left(\frac12\right)^x\), the coordinate axes, and \(x=2\). (a) Use two applications of the trapezoidal rule to estimate the area of the shaded region. (b) Show that the exact area of the shaded region is \(\frac{3}{4\ln2}\). (c) Using your answers from parts (a) and (b), deduce \(e<2\sqrt2\).
questions.answerLatex: Using two trapezia with width \(1\), the estimate is \(\frac12\left(1+2\cdot\frac12+\frac14\right)=\frac98\). The exact area is \(\int_0^2 2^{-x}\,dx=\left[-\frac{2^{-x}}{\ln2}\right]_0^2=\frac{3}{4\ln2}\). Since the graph is concave up, the exact area is less than the trapezoidal estimate, so \(\frac{3}{4\ln2}<\frac98\). Hence \(2<3\ln2=\ln8\), so \(e^2<8\) and \(e<2\sqrt2\).
workingLatex: The curve is concave up, so the trapezoidal estimate is greater than the exact area.

questions.title: Circular segment area and arc length
questions.promptLatex: A farmer wants to use a straight fence to divide a circular paddock of radius \(10\) metres into two segments. The smaller segment is \(\frac14\) of the paddock and is shaded in the diagram. The fence subtends an angle of \(\theta\) radians at the centre of the circle. (a) Show that \(\theta=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\). (b) The graph of \(y=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\) is shown. Use the graph and the result in part (a) to estimate the arc length of the smaller segment to the nearest metre.
questions.answerLatex: The shaded segment is the sector area minus the triangle area. Since it is one quarter of the circular paddock, \(\frac12(10)^2\theta-\frac12(10)^2\sin\theta=\frac14\pi(10)^2\). Hence \(50(\theta-\sin\theta)=25\pi\), so \(\theta=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\). From the graph, the intersection of \(y=\theta\) and \(y=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\) is about \(\theta=2.3\) radians. The arc length is \(r\theta\approx10\times2.3=23\) metres.
workingLatex: Area of shaded segment \(=\) area of sector \(-\) area of triangle.
Use the graph to estimate where \(y=	heta\) intersects \(y=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\), giving \(\theta\approx2.3\).

questions.title: Mountain height and bearing
questions.promptLatex: The point \(T\) is the peak of a mountain and the point \(O\) is directly below the mountain's peak. The point \(Y\) is due east of \(O\) and the angle of elevation of \(T\) from \(Y\) is \(60^\circ\). The point \(F\) is \(4\) km south-west of \(Y\). The points \(O\), \(Y\) and \(F\) are on level ground. The angle of elevation of \(T\) from \(F\) is \(45^\circ\). (a) Let the height of the mountain be \(h\). Show that \(OY=\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\). (b) Hence, or otherwise, find the value of \(h\), correct to 2 decimal places. (c) Find the bearing of point \(O\) from point \(F\), correct to the nearest degree.
questions.answerLatex: From \(Y\), \(\tan60^\circ=\frac{h}{OY}\), so \(OY=\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\). From \(F\), the angle of elevation is \(45^\circ\), so \(OF=h\). In \(\triangle FOY\), \(FY=4\) and \(\angle OYF=45^\circ\). By the cosine rule, \(h^2=\left(\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\right)^2+4^2-2\cdot\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\cdot4\cos45^\circ\). Solving gives \(h\approx3.03\) km. If \(\theta=\angle OFY\), then \(\frac{\sin\theta}{h/\sqrt3}=\frac{\sin45^\circ}{h}\), so \(\theta\approx24^\circ\). The bearing of \(O\) from \(F\) is \(45^\circ-24^\circ\approx021^\circ\).
workingLatex: Since \(\angle TFO=45^\circ\) and \(\angle TOF=90^\circ\), \(\triangle TFO\) is isosceles and \(OF=h\).

questions.title: Cosine equation parameter range
questions.promptLatex: The equation \(\cos(px)=\frac12\) has exactly two solutions for \(0\le x\le2\pi\), where \(p>0\). Find all possible values of \(p\).
questions.answerLatex: Let \(u=px\). Then \(0\le u\le2\pi p\), and \(\cos u=\frac12\) at \(u=\frac{\pi}{3},\frac{5\pi}{3},\frac{7\pi}{3},\ldots\). Exactly two solutions occur when \(\frac{5\pi}{3}\le2\pi p<\frac{7\pi}{3}\). Hence \(\frac56\le p<\frac76\).
workingLatex: \(u=px\) changes the interval to \(0\le u\le2\pi p\).
The first positive solutions of \(\cos u=\frac12\) are \(\frac{\pi}{3}\), \(\frac{5\pi}{3}\), and \(\frac{7\pi}{3}\).
For exactly two solutions, include \(\frac{5\pi}{3}\) but exclude \(\frac{7\pi}{3}\): \(\frac{5\pi}{3}\le2\pi p<\frac{7\pi}{3}\).

questions.title: Two-way counting with overlap
questions.promptLatex: In a group of \(60\) students, \(38\) play basketball, \(35\) play hockey and \(5\) do not play either basketball or hockey. How many students play both basketball and hockey? Options: A. \(55\), B. \(18\), C. \(13\), D. \(8\).
questions.answerLatex: B. Since \(5\) students play neither sport, \(55\) students play at least one sport. Therefore the overlap is \(38+35-55=18\).

questions.title: Normal comparison using z-scores
questions.promptLatex: Pia's marks in Year 10 assessments are shown. The scores for each subject were normally distributed. \[\begin{array}{c|ccc}&\text{Pia's mark}&\text{Year 10 mean}&\text{Year 10 standard deviation}\\\hline\text{English}&78&66&6\\\text{Mathematics}&80&71&10\\\text{Science}&77&70&15\\\text{History}&85&72&9\end{array}\] In which subject did Pia perform best in comparison with the rest of Year 10? Options: A. English, B. Mathematics, C. Science, D. History.
questions.answerLatex: A. English gives the largest \(z\)-score: \(\frac{78-66}{6}=2\).

questions.title: Parabola reflections
questions.promptLatex: The parabola \(y=(x-3)^2-2\) is reflected about the \(y\)-axis. This is then reflected about the \(x\)-axis. What is the equation of the resulting parabola? Options: A. \(y=(x+3)^2+2\), B. \(y=(x-3)^2+2\), C. \(y=-(x+3)^2+2\), D. \(y=-(x-3)^2+2\).
questions.answerLatex: C. Reflecting about the \(y\)-axis gives \(y=(-x-3)^2-2=(x+3)^2-2\). Reflecting this about the \(x\)-axis gives \(y=-(x+3)^2+2\).
workingLatex: A reflection in the \(y\)-axis replaces \(x\) by \(-x\).
A reflection in the \(x\)-axis multiplies the function value by \(-1\).

questions.title: Chain rule antiderivative
Domain of a rational square-root function
questions.promptLatex: What is the domain of the function \(f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^2-1}}\)? Options: A. \([-1,1]\), B. \(( -\infty,-1]\cup[1,\infty )\), C. \((-1,1)\), D. \(( -\infty,-1)\cup(1,\infty )\).
questions.answerLatex: D. The expression under the square root is in the denominator, so \(x^2-1\) must be strictly positive. Hence \(x<-1\) or \(x>1\).

questions.title: Function transformation from graph
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows the graph \(y=f(x)\). Which of the following best represents the graph \(y=f(2x-1)\)?
questions.answerLatex: C. The transformation \(y=f(2x-1)=f(2(x-\frac12))\) compresses the graph horizontally by a factor of \(\frac12\) and shifts it right by \(\frac12\). The transformed key \(x\)-values match option C.
workingLatex: If an original input is \(u\), then the transformed graph has \(u=2x-1\), so \(x=\frac{u+1}{2}\).
The original zeros at approximately \(0\), \(2\), and \(4\) move to approximately \(0.5\), \(1.5\), and \(2.5\).

questions.title: Conditional probability from a tree diagram
questions.promptLatex: A bag contains \(2\) red and \(3\) white marbles. Jovan randomly selects two marbles at the same time from this bag. Given that one of the marbles selected is red, what is the probability that the other marble selected is also red? Options: A. \(\frac1{10}\), B. \(\frac17\), C. \(\frac14\), D. \(\frac7{10}\).
questions.answerLatex: B. The probability of two red marbles is \(\frac25\cdot\frac14=\frac1{10}\). The probability of at least one red marble is \(1-\frac35\cdot\frac24=\frac7{10}\). Therefore \(P(\text{two red}\mid\text{at least one red})=\frac{1/10}{7/10}=\frac17\).

questions.title: Inflection points of an antiderivative
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows the graph \(y=f(x)\). The point \(Q\) is a horizontal point of inflection. Let \(A(x)=\int_0^x f(t)\,dt\). How many points of inflection does the graph \(y=A(x)\) have? Options: A. \(2\), B. \(3\), C. \(4\), D. \(5\).
questions.answerLatex: B. Since \(A'(x)=f(x)\), \(A''(x)=f'(x)\). Points of inflection of \(A\) occur where \(f'\) changes sign, which corresponds to the turning points of \(f\). The graph shows three such turning points; the horizontal point of inflection \(Q\) does not add another because \(f'\) does not change sign there.
workingLatex: Count where the gradient of \(f\) changes sign on the graph.

questions.title: Arithmetic series sum

questions.answerLatex: The first term is \(a=50\), the common difference is \(d=7\), and the last term is \(2024\). Solving \(50+(n-1)7=2024\) gives \(n=283\). Hence \(S_n=\frac{283}{2}(50+2024)=293471\).

questions.title: Capacitor voltage exponential model
questions.promptLatex: In a particular electrical circuit, the voltage \(V\) volts across a capacitor is given by \(V(t)=6.5(1-e^{-kt})\), where \(k\) is a positive constant and \(t\) is the number of seconds after the circuit is switched on. (a) Draw a sketch of the graph of \(V(t)\), showing its behaviour as \(t\) increases. (b) When \(t=1\), the voltage across the capacitor is \(2.6\) volts. Find the value of \(k\), correct to three decimal places. (c) Find the rate at which the voltage is increasing when \(t=2\), correct to three decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: The graph starts at \((0,0)\), increases, and approaches the horizontal asymptote \(V=6.5\). From \(2.6=6.5(1-e^{-k})\), \(e^{-k}=0.6\), so \(k=-\ln(0.6)\approx0.511\). Since \(\frac{dV}{dt}=6.5ke^{-kt}\), at \(t=2\) the rate is \(6.5ke^{-2k}\approx1.195\) volts per second.

questions.title: Goal attempt probability
questions.promptLatex: In a game, the probability that a particular player scores a goal at each attempt is \(0.15\). (a) What is the probability that this player does not score a goal in the first two attempts? (b) Determine the least number of attempts that this player must make so that the probability of scoring at least one goal is greater than \(0.8\).
questions.answerLatex: The probability of missing one attempt is \(0.85\), so the probability of missing the first two attempts is \((0.85)^2=0.7225\). For \(n\) attempts, \(P(\text{at least one goal})=1-(0.85)^n\). Requiring \(1-(0.85)^n>0.8\) gives \((0.85)^n<0.2\), so \(n>\frac{\ln0.2}{\ln0.85}\approx9.90\). Therefore the least number of attempts is \(10\).

questions.title: Sketching a quartic from derivatives
questions.promptLatex: Sketch the curve \(y=x^4-2x^3+2\) by first finding all stationary points, checking their nature, and finding the points of inflection.
questions.answerLatex: The derivatives are \(y'=4x^3-6x^2=2x^2(2x-3)\) and \(y''=12x^2-12x=12x(x-1)\). Stationary points occur at \(x=0\) and \(x=\frac32\). At \(x=0\), \(y=2\), and the concavity changes sign, so \((0,2)\) is a horizontal point of inflection. At \(x=\frac32\), \(y=\frac{5}{16}\), and \(y''>0\), so \(\left(\frac32,\frac5{16}\right)\) is a local minimum. The other point of inflection is \((1,1)\).
workingLatex: \(y''=12x^2-12x=12x(x-1)\), so possible inflection points are at \(x=0\) and \(x=1\).
At \(x=0\), \(y=2\), and the concavity changes sign, so \((0,2)\) is a horizontal point of inflection.
At \(x=\frac32\), \(y=\frac5{16}\), and \(y''=9>0\), so this point is a local minimum.
At \(x=1\), \(y=1\), giving the other point of inflection \((1,1)\).

betterResponses: find the first and second derivatives correctly
determine the nature of the stationary points using either the first or second derivative test

justify a change in concavity
clearly provide a sketch of the function.

questions.title: Tower bearing
questions.promptLatex: A vertical tower \(TC\) is \(40\) metres high. Point \(A\) is due east of \(C\), and the angle of elevation of \(T\) from \(A\) is \(35^\circ\). From another point \(B\), the angle of elevation of \(T\) is \(30^\circ\), and \(AB=100\) metres. (a) Show that \(AC=57.13\) metres, correct to two decimal places. (b) Find the bearing of \(B\) from \(C\), correct to the nearest degree.
questions.answerLatex: Since \(\tan35^\circ=\frac{40}{AC}\), \(AC=\frac{40}{\tan35^\circ}\approx57.13\) metres. Also \(BC=\frac{40}{\tan30^\circ}\approx69.28\) metres. Applying the cosine rule in \(\triangle ABC\) gives \(\angle ACB\approx104^\circ\), so the bearing of \(B\) from \(C\) is approximately \(194^\circ\).

questions.title: Concavity and trapezoidal rule
questions.promptLatex: The graph of the function \(f(x)=\ln(1+x^2)\) is shown. (a) Prove that \(f(x)\) is concave up for \(-1<x<1\). (b) A table of function values, correct to \(4\) decimal places, is provided: \[\begin{array}{c|ccccc}x&0&0.25&0.5&0.75&1\\\hline \ln(1+x^2)&0&0.0606&0.2231&0.4463&0.6931\end{array}\] Using the function values provided and the trapezoidal rule, estimate the shaded area in the diagram. (c) Is the answer to part (b) an overestimate or underestimate? Give a reason.
questions.answerLatex: For \(f(x)=\ln(1+x^2)\), \(f'(x)=\frac{2x}{1+x^2}\) and \(f''(x)=\frac{2(1-x^2)}{(1+x^2)^2}\), which is positive for \(-1<x<1\). Using symmetry and the trapezoidal rule on \([0,1]\), the shaded area is approximately \(0.538275\). This is an overestimate because the function is concave up, so the trapezoids lie above the curve.
workingLatex: For \(-1<x<1\), \(1-x^2>0\), and the denominator is always positive, so \(f''(x)>0\).
The function and shaded area are symmetric, so estimate twice the area from \(0\) to \(1\).
Because \(f\) is concave up on the interval, the straight line segments used in the trapezoidal rule lie above the curve.

questions.title: Normal table probabilities and percentiles
questions.promptLatex: A random variable is normally distributed with mean \(0\) and standard deviation \(1\). The table gives values of \(P(Z<z)\) for \(z=0.6,0.7,\ldots,1.4\), and the diagram represents the shaded area. The scores in a university examination are normally distributed with mean \(58\) and standard deviation \(15\). (a) By calculating a \(z\)-score, find the percentage of scores between \(58\) and \(70\). (b) Explain why the percentage of scores between \(46\) and \(70\) is twice your answer to part (a). (c) Using the table, find an approximate minimum score needed to be placed in the top \(10\%\) of candidates.
questions.answerLatex: For \(70\), \(z=\frac{70-58}{15}=0.8\). From the table, \(P(Z<0.8)=0.7881\), so \(P(58<X<70)=0.7881-0.5=0.2881\), or \(28.81\%\). The interval from \(46\) to \(58\) corresponds to \(-0.8<Z<0\), so by symmetry it has the same area as \(0<Z<0.8\). For the top \(10\%\), use \(P(Z<z)\approx0.9\), so \(z\approx1.3\) and \(x=58+1.3(15)=77.5\), about \(78\) as a whole-number score.
workingLatex: \(46\) has \(z=\frac{46-58}{15}=-0.8\), so the normal distribution's symmetry makes \(P(46<X<58)=P(58<X<70)\).
For the top \(10\%\), the cumulative probability is approximately \(0.9\). The table value closest to this is \(0.9032\) at \(z=1.3\).
\(x=58+1.3(15)=77.5\), so the required score is approximately \(78\) if scores are whole numbers.

questions.title: Monthly savings annuity
questions.promptLatex: Jack intends to deposit \(\$80\) into a savings account on the first day of each month for \(24\) months. The interest rate during this time is \(6\%\) per annum, compounded monthly. (a) Calculate how much money Jack will have in his account at the end of the \(24\) months. (b) A table of future value interest factors for an annuity of \(\$1\) could have been used for this calculation. For \(24\) periods at \(0.5\%\) interest per period, find the table value \(A\), correct to three decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: The monthly rate is \(0.005\). Because deposits are made on the first day of each month, the future value is \(80\left((1.005)^1+(1.005)^2+\cdots+(1.005)^{24}\right)=80(1.005)\frac{(1.005)^{24}-1}{0.005}\approx2044.73\). Since \(80A=2044.73\), \(A\approx25.559\).

questions.title: Probability density function and median
questions.promptLatex: A function \(f(x)\) is defined by \[f(x)=\begin{cases}0,&x<0,\\1-\frac{x}{h},&0\le x\le h,\\0,&x>h,\end{cases}\] where \(h\) is a constant. (a) Find the value of \(h\) such that \(f(x)\) is a probability density function. (b) By first finding a formula for the cumulative distribution function, sketch its graph. (c) Find the value of the median of the probability density function \(f(x)\), correct to three decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: For \(f\) to be a probability density function, the triangular area must be \(1\), so \(\frac12(1)(h)=1\), giving \(h=2\). For \(0\le x\le2\), \(F(x)=\int_0^x\left(1-\frac{t}{2}\right)dt=x-\frac{x^2}{4}\). Thus \(F(x)=0\) for \(x<0\), \(F(x)=x-\frac{x^2}{4}\) for \(0\le x\le2\), and \(F(x)=1\) for \(x>2\). The median \(m\) satisfies \(m-\frac{m^2}{4}=\frac12\), so \(m=2-\sqrt2\approx0.586\).

questions.title: Present value for staged withdrawals
questions.promptLatex: Twenty-five years ago, Phoenix deposited a single sum of money into a new bank account earning \(2.4\%\) interest per annum, compounded monthly. Present value interest factors for an annuity of \(\$1\) include \[\begin{array}{c|cccc}n\backslash r&0.001&0.002&0.003&0.004\\\hline60&58.207&56.487&54.835&53.249\\120&113.026&106.592&100.649&95.156\\180&164.655&151.036&138.927&128.137\\240&213.278&190.460&170.908&154.093\\300&259.071&225.430&197.627&174.521\end{array}\] Phoenix withdrew \(\$2000\) at the end of each month for the first \(15\) years, starting at the end of the first month, and \(\$1200\) at the end of each month for the next \(10\) years, starting at the end of the \(181\)st month. Calculate the minimum sum Phoenix could have deposited to make these withdrawals.
questions.answerLatex: The monthly interest rate is \(r=0.024/12=0.002\). The account must fund \(\$1200\) per month for \(300\) months, plus an extra \(\$800\) per month for the first \(180\) months. Using the table, the required present value is \(1200(225.430)+800(151.036)=270516+120828.80=\$391344.80\).
workingLatex: Model the withdrawals as \(\$1200\) for all \(300\) months plus an extra \(\$800\) for the first \(180\) months.
From the table, the factor for \(n=300\), \(r=0.002\) is \(225.430\).
From the table, the factor for \(n=180\), \(r=0.002\) is \(151.036\).

betterResponses: convert the time periods and interest rate to monthly values
identify the appropriate interest factors from the table
break down the calculations for the different withdrawals using values from the table
use geometric series to model the two different withdrawals.
improvementAreas: understanding that the table was provided to assist with calculations
understanding the concept of present value
completing more practice in solving financial problems using tables.

questions.title: Derivative and integral using tan x
questions.promptLatex: (a) Find the derivative of \(x^2\tan x\). (b) Hence, find \(\int (x\tan x+1)^2\,dx\).
questions.answerLatex: By the product rule, \(\frac{d}{dx}(x^2\tan x)=x^2\sec^2x+2x\tan x\). Expanding the integrand gives \((x\tan x+1)^2=x^2\tan^2x+2x\tan x+1=x^2\sec^2x+2x\tan x-x^2+1\). Hence \(\int (x\tan x+1)^2\,dx=x^2\tan x-\frac{x^3}{3}+x+C\).
workingLatex: Use \(\tan^2x=\sec^2x-1\) to write the integrand as \(x^2\sec^2x+2x\tan x-x^2+1\).

betterResponses: (a) identify the use of product rule

(b) substitute 𝑡𝑎𝑛2𝑥 = 𝑠𝑒𝑐2𝑥 − 1 correctly

questions.title: Ferris wheel height functions
questions.promptLatex: Anna is sitting in a carriage of a Ferris wheel which is revolving. The height, \(A(t)\), in metres above the ground of the top of her carriage is given by \(A(t)=c-k\cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{24}\right)\), where \(t\) is the time in seconds after Anna's carriage first reaches the bottom of its revolution and \(c\) and \(k\) are constants. The top of each carriage reaches a greatest height of \(39\) metres and a smallest height of \(3\) metres. (a) Find \(c\) and \(k\). (b) How many seconds does it take for one complete revolution of the Ferris wheel? (c) Billie is in another carriage with height \(B(t)=c-k\cos\left(\frac{\pi(t-6)}{24}\right)\). During each revolution, there are two occasions when Anna's and Billie's carriages are at the same heights. At what two heights does this occur? Give your answer correct to \(2\) decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: The maximum and minimum heights give \(c+k=39\) and \(c-k=3\), so \(c=21\) and \(k=18\). The period is \(\frac{2\pi}{\pi/24}=48\) seconds. Solving \(A(t)=B(t)\) over one revolution gives \(t=3\) and \(t=27\), so the matching heights are \(21-18\cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{24}\right)\approx4.37\) metres and \(21-18\cos\left(\frac{27\pi}{24}\right)\approx37.63\) metres.
workingLatex: Over one revolution, the relevant solutions are \(t=3\) and \(t=27\).

questions.title: Quadratic tangent and normal
questions.promptLatex: Consider the curve \(y=ax^2+bx+c\), where \(a>0\). At a particular point, the tangent and normal to the curve are given by \(t(x)=2x+3\) and \(n(x)=-\frac12x-2\), respectively. The curve has a minimum turning point at \(x=-4\). Find the values of \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\).
questions.answerLatex: The tangent and normal meet where \(2x+3=-\frac12x-2\), so \(x=-2\) and \(y=-1\). For \(y=ax^2+bx+c\), \(y'=2ax+b\). The turning point at \(x=-4\) gives \(-8a+b=0\), so \(b=8a\). The tangent gradient at \(x=-2\) is \(2\), giving \(-4a+b=2\). Hence \(a=\frac12\), \(b=4\), and substituting \((-2,-1)\) gives \(c=5\).
workingLatex: At \(x=-2\), the tangent gradient is \(2\), so \(-4a+b=2\).

betterResponses: differentiate the equation of the curve to get an expression for the gradient of the tangent, identify the gradient of the tangent from its equation and link this to their expression
use the stationary points and the tangent/normal gradients to form 2 equations in terms of a and b and then solve them simultaneously
solve equation of the normal and tangent simultaneous to find the point of intersection as (−2,1)
use the gradient of the derived function at 𝑥 = −2 equals 2 to show that −4𝑎 + 𝑏 = 2
solve −8𝑎 + 𝑏 = 0 and −4𝑎 + 𝑏 = 2 simultaneously to find 𝑎 = 1 2 and 𝑏 = 4.
improvementAreas: finding solutions of simultaneous equations
equating the derivative equal to zero after substituting in 𝑥 = −4
checking their solution after finding 𝑎, 𝑏 and 𝑐
understanding that at a minimum turning point, the first derivative is equal to zero
understanding that the tangent and normal intersect at the point where the tangent touches the curve.

questions.title: Range of a geometric series limiting sum
questions.promptLatex: Suppose the geometric series \(x+x^2+x^3+\cdots\) has a limiting sum \(S\). By considering the graph \(y=-1-\frac{1}{x-1}\), or otherwise, find the range of possible values of \(S\).
questions.answerLatex: For the geometric series to have a limiting sum, \(-1<x<1\). The sum is \(S=\frac{x}{1-x}\). Since \(-1<x<1\), the graph \(y=\frac{x}{1-x}=-1-\frac{1}{x-1}\) has range \(S>-\frac12\).
workingLatex: At \(x=-1\), the boundary value is \(-\frac12\), and this endpoint is excluded.

betterResponses: find the limiting sum and interpret the restriction on the common ratio
show the equivalence of equations involving fractions with different denominators

improvementAreas: using the reference sheet to find the limiting sum and understand the restriction.

questions.title: Annular sector perimeter optimisation
questions.promptLatex: Two circles have the same centre \(O\). The smaller circle has radius \(1\) cm, while the larger circle has radius \(1+x\) cm. The circles enclose a shaded region \(QRST\), which is subtended by an angle \(\theta\) at \(O\). The area of \(QRST\) is \(A\text{ cm}^2\), where \(A\) is a constant and \(A>0\). Let \(P\) cm be the perimeter of \(QRST\). (a) By finding expressions for the area and perimeter of \(QRST\), show that \(P(x)=2x+\frac{2A}{x}\). (b) Show that if the perimeter \(P(x)\) is minimised, then \(\theta\) must be less than \(2\).
questions.answerLatex: The perimeter is \(P=(1+x)\theta+\theta+2x=(2+x)\theta+2x\). The area is \(A=\frac12(1+x)^2\theta-\frac12\theta=\frac12\theta x(2+x)\), so \((2+x)\theta=\frac{2A}{x}\) and \(P(x)=2x+\frac{2A}{x}\). For a minimum, \(P'(x)=2-\frac{2A}{x^2}=0\), so \(x=\sqrt A\), and \(P''(x)=\frac{4A}{x^3}>0\). Substituting \(x=\sqrt A\) into \(A=\frac12\theta x(2+x)\) gives \(\theta=\frac{2\sqrt A}{2+\sqrt A}<2\).

questions.title: Arithmetic sequence term
questions.promptLatex: The first three terms of an arithmetic sequence are \(3\), \(7\) and \(11\). Find the \(15\)th term.
questions.answerLatex: The common difference is \(d=7-3=4\). With first term \(a=3\), the \(15\)th term is \(T_{15}=a+(15-1)d=3+14\times4=59\).

questions.title: Tangent to a cubic composite
questions.promptLatex: Find the equation of the tangent to the curve \(y=(2x+1)^3\) at the point \((0,1)\).
questions.answerLatex: The derivative is \(y'=3(2x+1)^2\cdot2=6(2x+1)^2\). At \(x=0\), the gradient is \(6\). The tangent through \((0,1)\) is \(y-1=6(x-0)\), so \(y=6x+1\).

questions.title: Annuity future value factors
questions.promptLatex: A table of future value interest factors for an annuity of \(\$1\) is shown. \[\begin{array}{c|cccc}\text{Period}\backslash\text{Rate}&1.5\%&3\%&4.5\%&6\%\\\hline5&5.152&5.309&5.471&5.637\\10&10.703&11.464&12.288&13.181\\20&23.124&26.870&31.371&36.786\\40&54.268&75.401&107.030&154.762\end{array}\] (a) Micky wants to save \(\$450\,000\) over the next \(10\) years. If the interest rate is \(6\%\) per annum compounding annually, how much should Micky contribute each year? Give your answer to the nearest dollar. (b) Instead, Micky decides to contribute \(\$8535\) every three months for \(10\) years to an annuity paying \(6\%\) per annum, compounding quarterly. How much will Micky have at the end of \(10\) years?
questions.answerLatex: For part (a), use the factor \(13.181\), giving \(450000\div13.181\approx\$34\,140\). For part (b), the quarterly rate is \(1.5\%\) and there are \(40\) periods, so use the factor \(54.268\). The amount is \(8535\times54.268=\$463\,177.38\).

questions.title: Rectangle and arc perimeter
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows a shape \(APQBCD\). The shape consists of a rectangle \(ABCD\), with an arc \(PQ\) on side \(AB\). The side lengths are \(BC=3.6\text{ m}\) and \(CD=8.0\text{ m}\). The arc \(PQ\) is an arc of a circle with centre \(O\), radius \(2.1\text{ m}\), and \(\angle POQ=110^\circ\). What is the perimeter of the shape \(APQBCD\)? Give your answer correct to one decimal place.
questions.answerLatex: The arc length is \(\frac{110}{360}\times2\pi(2.1)\approx4.0317\). The chord \(PQ\) has length \(\sqrt{2.1^2+2.1^2-2(2.1)(2.1)\cos110^\circ}\approx3.4404\). Hence the perimeter is \(2(3.6)+8.0+(8.0-3.4404)+4.0317\approx23.8\text{ m}\).

set their working out clearly and identify the parts they were calculating, and show their rounded answers to at least three decimal places to assist the calculation for the perimeter
identify the arc length was 110/360 of a circle and not a semi-circle
correctly substitute values into the correct formulas (the sine or cosine rule for line segment PQ and arc length formula for arc PQ) and show full substitution
round the final solution to 1 decimal place.

understanding that the question requires the calculation of the perimeter of the shape, therefore no area formulas were needed

questions.title: Gas usage least-squares regression
questions.subtopic: Descriptive statistics and bivariate data analysis
questions.promptLatex: A university uses gas to heat its buildings. Over \(10\) weekdays, the average outside temperatures were \(0^\circ,0^\circ,0^\circ,2^\circ,5^\circ,7^\circ,8^\circ,9^\circ,9^\circ,10^\circ\), and the total gas usage for the ten weekdays was \(1840\text{ MW}\). The least-squares regression line predicts that when the temperature is \(0^\circ\text{C}\), the daily gas usage is \(236\text{ MW}\). In any bivariate dataset, the least-squares regression line passes through \((\bar{x},\bar{y})\). (a) Plot \((\bar{x},\bar{y})\) and the \(y\)-intercept of the least-squares regression line on the grid. (b) What is the equation of the regression line? (c) In context, identify one problem with using the regression line to predict gas usage when the average outside temperature is \(23^\circ\text{C}\).
questions.answerLatex: The means are \(\bar{x}=5\) and \(\bar{y}=184\), so the line passes through \((5,184)\) and has \(y\)-intercept \((0,236)\). The slope is \(\frac{184-236}{5}=-10.4\), so the regression equation is \(y=236-10.4x\). At \(x=23\), the model gives a negative gas usage, which is not physically possible.
workingLatex: The two plotted points are \((5,184)\) and \((0,236)\).
\(x=23\) is outside the observed temperature range and the equation predicts negative usage.

questions.title: Shifted sine equation
questions.promptLatex: Find all values of \(\theta\), where \(0^\circ\le\theta\le360^\circ\), such that \(\sin(\theta-60^\circ)=-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\).
questions.answerLatex: Since \(-60^\circ\le\theta-60^\circ\le300^\circ\), the relevant solutions of \(\sin u=-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\) are \(u=-60^\circ\), \(240^\circ\), and \(300^\circ\). Therefore \(\theta=0^\circ\), \(300^\circ\), and \(360^\circ\).

questions.title: Geometric sequence common ratio
questions.promptLatex: The fourth term of a geometric sequence is \(48\). The eighth term of the same sequence is \(\frac{3}{16}\). Find the possible value(s) of the common ratio and the corresponding first term(s).
questions.answerLatex: Let the first term be \(a\) and the common ratio be \(r\). Then \(ar^3=48\) and \(ar^7=\frac{3}{16}\). Dividing gives \(r^4=\frac{1}{256}\), so \(r=\pm\frac14\). If \(r=\frac14\), then \(a=3072\). If \(r=-\frac14\), then \(a=-3072\).

betterResponses: use the Reference Sheet to identify and use the correct Geometric Progression formulas
solve simultaneous equations involving index notation
identify the positive and negative values for the common ratio and the respective first terms.
improvementAreas: understanding the differences between an Arithmetic and Geometric sequence
understanding that when solving an equation with an ‘even power’, that there will be two possible answers
making ‘𝑟𝑟’ the subject complicates the simultaneous equation solution.

questions.title: Angle in a rectangular prism
questions.promptLatex: In the rectangular prism shown, \(AD=7\) cm, \(AE=8\) cm and \(EF=6\) cm. Point \(M\) is the midpoint of \(CD\). Find \(\angle AEM\), to the nearest degree.
questions.answerLatex: Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(CD\), \(DM=3\) cm. In \(\triangle ADM\), \(AM^2=7^2+3^2=58\), so \(AM=\sqrt{58}\). In \(\triangle AEM\), \(\tan \angle AEM=\frac{\sqrt{58}}{8}\). Therefore \(\angle AEM\approx43.59^\circ\), which is \(44^\circ\) to the nearest degree.

betterResponses: efficiently find the angle AEM by only finding AM and then using tangent ratio

questions.title: Koala weights normal distribution
questions.promptLatex: A random variable is normally distributed with mean \(0\) and standard deviation \(1\). The table gives \(P(Z<z)\) for positive values of \(z\): \[\begin{array}{c|cccccccccc}z&1.30&1.31&1.32&1.33&1.34&1.35&1.36&1.37&1.38&1.39\\\hline P(Z<z)&0.9032&0.9049&0.9066&0.9082&0.9099&0.9115&0.9131&0.9147&0.9162&0.9177\end{array}\] The weights of adult male koalas form a normal distribution with mean \(\mu=10.40\) kg and standard deviation \(\sigma=1.15\) kg. In a group of \(400\) adult male koalas, how many would be expected to weigh more than \(11.93\) kg?
questions.answerLatex: The standardised value is \(z=\frac{11.93-10.40}{1.15}=1.33\). From the table, \(P(Z<1.33)=0.9082\), so \(P(X>11.93)=1-0.9082=0.0918\). The expected number is \(400\times0.0918=36.72\), so about \(37\) koalas would be expected.
workingLatex: \(400\times0.0918=36.72\), so the expected number is approximately \(37\).

questions.title: Minimum area of a garden path
questions.promptLatex: A gardener wants to build a rectangular garden of area \(50\text{ m}^2\) against an existing wall. A concrete path of width \(1\) metre is to be built around the other three sides of the garden. Let \(x\) and \(y\) be the dimensions, in metres, of the outer rectangle as shown. (a) Show that \(y=\frac{50}{x-2}+1\). (b) Find the value of \(x\) such that the area of the concrete path is a minimum. Show that your answer gives a minimum area.
questions.answerLatex: The garden dimensions are \((x-2)\) by \((y-1)\), so \(50=(x-2)(y-1)\) and \(y=\frac{50}{x-2}+1\). The path area is \(A=xy-50=x\left(\frac{50}{x-2}+1\right)-50=\frac{100}{x-2}+x\). Then \(A'=1-\frac{100}{(x-2)^2}\). Setting \(A'=0\) gives \((x-2)^2=100\), so \(x=12\) for \(x>2\). Also \(A''=\frac{200}{(x-2)^3}>0\) at \(x=12\), so the area is minimised when \(x=12\).
workingLatex: \(A''=\frac{200}{(x-2)^3}\), so \(A''(12)>0\) and the stationary point is a minimum.

questions.title: Swing motion distance model
questions.promptLatex: A camera films the motion of a swing in a park. Let \(x(t)\) be the horizontal distance, in metres, from the camera to the seat of the swing at \(t\) seconds. The seat is released from rest at a horizontal distance of \(11.2\) m from the camera. (a) The rate of change of \(x\) can be modelled by \(\frac{dx}{dt}=-1.5\pi\sin\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)\). Find an expression for \(x(t)\). (b) How many times does the swing reach the closest point to the camera during the first \(10\) seconds?
questions.answerLatex: Integrating gives \(x(t)=1.2\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)+C\). Since \(x(0)=11.2\), \(11.2=1.2+C\), so \(C=10\) and \(x(t)=1.2\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)+10\). The period is \(\frac{2\pi}{5\pi/4}=1.6\) seconds. The closest positions occur at the minima, first at \(t=0.8\) and then every \(1.6\) seconds, so this happens \(6\) times in the first \(10\) seconds.
workingLatex: The closest point is reached at \(t=0.8,2.4,4.0,5.6,7.2,8.8\), so \(6\) times.

questions.title: Absolute value graph parameters
questions.promptLatex: The graph of \(y=f(x)\), where \(f(x)=a|x-b|+c\), passes through the points \((3,-5)\), \((6,7)\) and \((9,-5)\) as shown in the diagram. (a) Find the values of \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\). (b) The line \(y=mx\) cuts the graph of \(y=f(x)\) in two distinct places. Find all possible values of \(m\).
questions.answerLatex: The vertex is \((6,7)\), so \(b=6\) and \(c=7\). Using \((3,-5)\), \(-5=a|3-6|+7=3a+7\), so \(a=-4\). For \(y=mx\) to cut the graph twice, its slope must be less than the slope from the origin to the vertex, \(\frac76\), and greater than the slope of the right branch, \(-4\). Hence \(-4<m<\frac76\).
workingLatex: The graph has vertex \((6,7)\), so \(b=6\) and \(c=7\).
The boundary slope through the origin and vertex is \(\frac{7}{6}\).
Therefore the line cuts the graph twice for \(-4<m<\frac76\).

questions.title: Parallel tangent point on a curve
questions.promptLatex: The curve \(y=f(x)\) is shown on the diagram. The equation of the tangent to the curve at point \(T(-1,6)\) is \(y=x+7\). At a point \(R\), another tangent parallel to the tangent at \(T\) is drawn. The gradient function of the curve is \(\frac{dy}{dx}=3x^2-6x-8\). Find the coordinates of \(R\).
questions.answerLatex: Parallel tangents have the same gradient, so solve \(3x^2-6x-8=1\). This gives \(3x^2-6x-9=0\), so \(x=-1\) or \(x=3\). The point \(R\) has \(x=3\). Since \(y=x^3-3x^2-8x+C\), and \(6=(-1)^3-3(-1)^2-8(-1)+C\), \(C=2\). Thus \(y(3)=27-27-24+2=-22\), so \(R=(3,-22)\).
workingLatex: The second tangent point is at \(x=3\).

questions.title: Continuous random variable mode and CDF
questions.promptLatex: A continuous random variable \(X\) has probability density function \(f(x)=\begin{cases}12x^2(1-x),&0\le x\le1,\\0,&\text{otherwise.}\end{cases}\) (a) Find the mode of \(X\). (b) Find the cumulative distribution function for the given probability density function. (c) Without calculating the median, show that the mode is greater than the median.
questions.answerLatex: For \(0\le x\le1\), \(f(x)=12x^2-12x^3\), so \(f'(x)=24x-36x^2=12x(2-3x)\). The interior maximum occurs at \(x=\frac23\), so the mode is \(\frac23\). The CDF is \(F(x)=0\) for \(x<0\), \(F(x)=\int_0^x12t^2(1-t)\,dt=4x^3-3x^4\) for \(0\le x\le1\), and \(F(x)=1\) for \(x>1\). Since \(F\left(\frac23\right)=4\left(\frac{8}{27}\right)-3\left(\frac{16}{81}\right)=\frac{16}{27}>\frac12\), the median is less than \(\frac23\), so the mode is greater than the median.
workingLatex: More than half the probability lies below the mode, so the median is less than the mode.

questions.title: Stationary points of a damped sine curve
questions.promptLatex: Let \(f(x)=e^{-x}\sin x\). (a) Find the coordinates of the stationary points of \(f(x)\) for \(0\le x\le2\pi\). You do not need to check the nature of the stationary points. (b) Without using any further calculus, sketch the graph of \(y=f(x)\), for \(0\le x\le2\pi\), showing stationary points and intercepts.
questions.answerLatex: Using the product rule, \(f'(x)=e^{-x}\cos x-e^{-x}\sin x=e^{-x}(\cos x-\sin x)\). Since \(e^{-x}\ne0\), stationary points occur when \(\cos x=\sin x\), so \(x=\frac\pi4\) or \(x=\frac{5\pi}{4}\). The points are \(\left(\frac\pi4,\frac{e^{-\pi/4}}{\sqrt2}\right)\) and \(\left(\frac{5\pi}{4},-\frac{e^{-5\pi/4}}{\sqrt2}\right)\). Intercepts occur at \(x=0,\pi,2\pi\).

questions.title: Conditional probability and availability
questions.promptLatex: Four Year 12 students want to organise a graduation party. All four students have the same probability \(P(F)\) of being available next Friday and the same probability \(P(S)\) of being available next Saturday. It is given that \(P(F)=\frac{3}{10}\), \(P(S\mid F)=\frac13\), and \(P(F\mid S)=\frac18\). Kim is one of the four students. (a) Is Kim's availability next Friday independent from his availability next Saturday? Justify your answer. (b) Show that the probability that Kim is available next Saturday is \(\frac45\). (c) What is the probability that at least one of the four students is not available next Saturday?
questions.answerLatex: Kim's availability is not independent because \(P(F\mid S)=\frac18\ne\frac{3}{10}=P(F)\). Also \(P(S\cap F)=P(S\mid F)P(F)=\frac13\times\frac{3}{10}=\frac{1}{10}\). Since \(P(F\mid S)=\frac{P(F\cap S)}{P(S)}=\frac18\), \(P(S)=\frac{4}{5}\). Therefore the probability that at least one of the four students is not available next Saturday is \(1-\left(\frac45\right)^4=\frac{369}{625}\).
workingLatex: \(P(F\mid S)=\frac18\ne\frac{3}{10}=P(F)\), so the events are not independent.

(b) calculate the probability intersection of Saturday and Friday.
(c) find the complement of all four students available.

questions.title: Exponential curve area and intersections
questions.promptLatex: The curves \(y=e^{-2x}\) and \(y=e^{-x}-\frac14\) intersect at exactly one point as shown in the diagram. The point of intersection has coordinates \(\left(\ln2,\frac14\right)\). Do not prove this. (a) Show that the area bounded by the two curves and the \(y\)-axis, as shaded in the diagram, is \(\frac14\ln2-\frac18\). (b) Find the values of \(k\) such that the curves \(y=e^{-2x}\) and \(y=e^{-x}+k\) intersect at two points.
questions.answerLatex: The shaded area is \(\int_0^{\ln2}\left(e^{-2x}-e^{-x}+\frac14\right)dx\). This equals \(\left[-\frac12e^{-2x}+e^{-x}+\frac{x}{4}\right]_0^{\ln2}=\frac14\ln2-\frac18\). For part (b), let \(u=e^{-x}>0\). The intersections satisfy \(u^2=u+k\), or \(u^2-u-k=0\). Two positive roots require \(1+4k>0\) and \(1-\sqrt{1+4k}>0\), so \(-\frac14<k<0\).
workingLatex: Two positive roots require \(1+4k>0\) and \(\sqrt{1+4k}<1\), giving \(-\frac14<k<0\).

questions.title: Two game win probability
questions.promptLatex: Liam is playing two games. He is equally likely to win each game. The probability that Liam will win at least one of the games is \(80\%\). Which of the following is closest to the probability that Liam will win both games? Options: A. \(31\%\), B. \(40\%\), C. \(55\%\), D. \(64\%\).
questions.answerLatex: A. The probability is closest to \(31\%\).
workingLatex: Let \(p\) be the probability Liam wins either game.
Assuming the games are independent, \(P(\text{at least one win})=1-(1-p)^2=0.8\).
The probability of winning both games is \(p^2=(1-\sqrt{0.2})^2\approx0.306\), closest to \(31\%\).

questions.title: Composite function graph options
questions.promptLatex: The graphs of \(y=f(x)\) and \(y=g(x)\) are shown. Which graph best represents \(y=g(f(x))\)? Options A-D are shown in the diagram.
questions.answerLatex: B.
workingLatex: The graph of \(f(x)\) is non-negative and has its minimum at \(x=0\), where \(f(0)=0\).
The input to \(g\) is therefore \(f(x)\ge0\), giving a graph symmetric about the \(y\)-axis through the even input pattern of \(f\).
Option B matches the resulting behaviour shown by \(g(f(x))\).

questions.title: Inverse variation melting time model
questions.promptLatex: A student believes that the time it takes for an ice cube to melt, \(M\) minutes, varies inversely with the room temperature, \(T^\circ\text{C}\). The student observes that at a room temperature of \(15^\circ\text{C}\), it takes \(12\) minutes for an ice cube to melt. (a) Find the equation relating \(M\) and \(T\). (b) By first completing the table of values, graph the relationship between temperature and time from \(T=5^\circ\text{C}\) to \(T=30^\circ\text{C}\).
questions.answerLatex: Inverse variation gives \(M=\frac{k}{T}\). Using \(T=15\) and \(M=12\), \(12=\frac{k}{15}\), so \(k=180\) and \(M=\frac{180}{T}\). The completed values are \(M=36\) when \(T=5\), \(M=12\) when \(T=15\), and \(M=6\) when \(T=30\).

questions.title: Trapezoidal rule approximation
questions.promptLatex: Use two applications of the trapezoidal rule to find an approximate value of \(\int_0^2\sqrt{1+x^2}\,dx\). Give your answer correct to \(2\) decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: Using two equal subintervals, \(h=1\). The function values are \(f(0)=1\), \(f(1)=\sqrt2\), and \(f(2)=\sqrt5\). Thus \(\int_0^2\sqrt{1+x^2}\,dx\approx\frac{1}{2}\left(1+2\sqrt2+\sqrt5\right)\approx3.03\).

questions.title: Special die conditional probability
questions.promptLatex: In a bag there are \(3\) six-sided dice. Two of the dice have faces marked \(1,2,3,4,5,6\). The other is a special die with faces marked \(1,2,3,5,5,5\). One die is randomly selected and tossed. (a) What is the probability that the die shows a \(5\)? (b) Given that the die shows a \(5\), what is the probability that it is the special die?
questions.answerLatex: For part (a), \(P(5)=\frac23\times\frac16+\frac13\times\frac36=\frac{5}{18}\). For part (b), \(P(\text{special}\mid5)=\frac{P(\text{special}\cap5)}{P(5)}=\frac{\frac13\times\frac36}{\frac{5}{18}}=\frac35\).

questions.title: Area enclosed by a parabola and line
questions.promptLatex: The parabola \(y=x^2\) meets the line \(y=2x+3\) at the points \((-1,1)\) and \((3,9)\), as shown in the diagram. Find the area enclosed by the parabola and the line.
questions.answerLatex: The line is above the parabola on \([-1,3]\), so the area is \(\int_{-1}^{3}(2x+3-x^2)\,dx\). This equals \(\left[x^2+3x-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^{3}=\frac{32}{3}\), or \(10\frac23\) square units.

questions.title: House of cards arithmetic series
questions.promptLatex: Cards are stacked to build a house of cards. A house of cards requires \(3\) cards in the top row, \(6\) cards in the next row, and each successive row has \(3\) more cards than the previous row. (a) Show that a house of cards with \(12\) rows has a total of \(234\) cards. (b) Another house of cards has a total of \(828\) cards. How many rows are in this house of cards?
questions.answerLatex: The card counts form an arithmetic series with \(a=3\) and \(d=3\). For \(12\) rows, \(S_{12}=\frac{12}{2}(2\cdot3+11\cdot3)=234\). For \(828\) cards, \(\frac{n}{2}(2\cdot3+(n-1)3)=828\), so \(3n(n+1)=1656\). Hence \(n^2+n-552=0\), giving \(n=23\) rows.

questions.title: Transformed quadratic parameters
questions.promptLatex: The graph of the function \(f(x)=x^2\) is translated \(m\) units to the right, dilated vertically by a scale factor of \(k\), and then translated \(5\) units down. The equation of the transformed function is \(g(x)=3x^2-12x+7\). Find the values of \(m\) and \(k\).
questions.answerLatex: The transformations give \(g(x)=k(x-m)^2-5\). Completing the square, \(3x^2-12x+7=3(x^2-4x)+7=3(x-2)^2-5\). Hence \(m=2\) and \(k=3\).

questions.title: Bacteria exponential growth model
questions.promptLatex: A scientist is studying the growth of bacteria. The scientist models the number of bacteria, \(N\), by \(N(t)=200e^{0.013t}\), where \(t\) is the number of hours after starting the experiment. (a) What is the initial number of bacteria in the experiment? (b) What is the number of bacteria \(24\) hours after starting the experiment? (c) What is the rate of increase in the number of bacteria \(24\) hours after starting the experiment?
questions.answerLatex: Initially, \(N(0)=200\). After \(24\) hours, \(N(24)=200e^{0.013\times24}\approx273\). The derivative is \(\frac{dN}{dt}=200(0.013)e^{0.013t}=0.013N(t)\), so at \(t=24\), \(\frac{dN}{dt}\approx0.013(273.23)\approx3.55\) bacteria per hour.

questions.title: Investment options future value comparison
questions.promptLatex: Eli is choosing between two investment options. Option 1 is depositing a single amount of \(\$40\,000\) today, earning interest of \(1.2\%\) per annum, compounded monthly. Option 2 is depositing \(\$1000\) at the end of each quarter, earning interest of \(2.4\%\) per annum, compounded quarterly. A table of future value interest factors for an annuity of \(\$1\) is shown. (a) What is the value of Eli's investment after \(10\) years using Option 1? (b) What is the difference between the future values after \(10\) years using Option 1 and Option 2?
questions.answerLatex: For Option 1, the monthly rate is \(0.012/12=0.001\) and there are \(120\) months, so \(FV=40000(1.001)^{120}\approx\$45\,097.17\). For Option 2, there are \(40\) quarters and the quarterly rate is \(0.024/4=0.006\), so the table factor is \(45.05630\). Thus \(FV=1000(45.05630)=\$45\,056.30\). The difference is \(\$40.87\).

questions.title: Global extrema of a cubic on an interval
questions.promptLatex: Find the global maximum and minimum values of \(y=x^3-6x^2+8\), where \(-1\le x\le7\).
questions.answerLatex: Differentiate to get \(\frac{dy}{dx}=3x^2-12x=3x(x-4)\), so stationary points occur at \(x=0\) and \(x=4\). Check these and the endpoints: \(y(-1)=1\), \(y(0)=8\), \(y(4)=-24\), and \(y(7)=57\). The global minimum value is \(-24\), and the global maximum value is \(57\).
workingLatex: Therefore the global minimum is \(-24\), and the global maximum is \(57\).

questions.title: Tide depth trigonometric model
questions.promptLatex: The depth of water in a bay rises and falls with the tide. On a particular day the depth of the water, \(d\) metres, can be modelled by \(d=1.3-0.6\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)\), where \(t\) is the time in hours since low tide. (a) Find the depth of water at low tide and at high tide. (b) What is the time interval, in hours, between two successive low tides? (c) For how long between successive low tides will the depth of water be at least \(1\) metre?
questions.answerLatex: The minimum depth is \(1.3-0.6=0.7\) m and the maximum depth is \(1.3+0.6=1.9\) m. The period is \(\frac{2\pi}{4\pi/25}=12.5\) hours. For \(d=1\), \(1=1.3-0.6\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)\), so \(\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)=\frac12\). Between successive low tides, this occurs at \(t=\frac{25}{12}\) and \(t=\frac{125}{12}\), so the depth is at least \(1\) metre for \(\frac{125}{12}-\frac{25}{12}=\frac{25}{3}=8\frac13\) hours.

questions.title: Actors and teenage characters bivariate data

questions.promptLatex: Jo is researching the relationship between the ages of teenage characters in television series and the ages of actors playing these characters. questions.answerLatex: The data show a positive approximately linear relationship, but the correlation coefficient \(0.4564\) indicates only a moderate association. The line of best fit suggests that for each additional year of character age, the actor age increases by about \(1.85\) years. Actors playing teenage characters range from about \(14\) to \(30\) years old, so actors do not need to be teenagers themselves. The widest spread of actor ages appears for characters aged \(15\), and characters older than \(15\) are generally played by older actors.
workingLatex: The association is positive and roughly linear.
\(r=0.4564\) indicates a moderate relationship rather than a strong one.
The fitted slope \(1.85\) means predicted actor age increases by about \(1.85\) years per additional year of character age.
The context shows teenage characters can be played by actors substantially older than the characters.

questions.title: Sine derivative conditions
questions.promptLatex: Let \(f(x)=\sin(2x)\). Find the value of \(x\), for \(0<x<\pi\), for which \(f'(x)=-\sqrt3\) and \(f''(x)=2\).
questions.answerLatex: Since \(f'(x)=2\cos(2x)\), \(2\cos(2x)=-\sqrt3\), so \(2x=\frac{5\pi}{6}\) or \(\frac{7\pi}{6}\). Since \(f''(x)=-4\sin(2x)\), \(-4\sin(2x)=2\), so \(\sin(2x)=-\frac12\), giving \(2x=\frac{7\pi}{6}\) or \(\frac{11\pi}{6}\). The common solution is \(2x=\frac{7\pi}{6}\), so \(x=\frac{7\pi}{12}\).

questions.title: Exponential product curve
questions.promptLatex: Let \(f(x)=xe^{-2x}\). It is given that \(f'(x)=e^{-2x}-2xe^{-2x}\). (a) Show that \(f''(x)=4(x-1)e^{-2x}\). (b) Find the stationary point on the graph of \(y=f(x)\) and determine its nature. (c) Sketch the curve \(y=f(x)\), showing the stationary point, the point of inflection and any intercepts.
questions.answerLatex: \(f''(x)=4(x-1)e^{-2x}\). The stationary point is \(\left(\frac12,\frac{1}{2e}\right)\), and it is a local maximum. The curve passes through \((0,0)\), has a point of inflection at \((1,e^{-2})\), and approaches the \(x\)-axis as \(x\to\infty\).
workingLatex: For a stationary point, \(f'(x)=0\Rightarrow 1-2x=0\Rightarrow x=\frac12\), giving \(y=\frac{1}{2e}\).
\(f''\left(\frac12\right)=4\left(-\frac12\right)e^{-1}<0\), so the stationary point is a local maximum.
The point of inflection occurs when \(f''(x)=0\), so \(x=1\) and \(y=e^{-2}\).

questions.title: Circle and hyperbola area
questions.promptLatex: The circle \(x^2+y^2=2\) has centre \(O\). The interval from \(O\) to the point \((1,1)\) makes an angle \(\theta\) with the positive \(x\)-axis. (a) Find the exact area of the shaded circular segment shown. A hyperbola with equation \(y=\frac{a}{b-x}-1\) passes through \((0,0)\) and \((1,1)\), with part of the region bounded by the hyperbola and circle shaded. (b) Show that \(a=b=2\). (c) Find the exact area of the shaded region bounded by the hyperbola and the circle.
questions.answerLatex: The circular segment area is \(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac12\). The hyperbola parameters are \(a=b=2\). The final shaded area is \(2\ln 2+\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac32\).
workingLatex: The sector has radius \(\sqrt2\) and angle \(\frac{\pi}{4}\), so its area is \(\frac12(\sqrt2)^2\cdot\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4}\).
The right triangle under the radius to \((1,1)\) has area \(\frac12\), giving segment area \(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac12\).
Substituting \((0,0)\) gives \(0=\frac{a}{b}-1\), so \(a=b\). Substituting \((1,1)\) then gives \(1=\frac{b}{b-1}-1\), so \(b=2\) and \(a=2\).
The area under the hyperbola from \(0\) to \(1\) is \(\int_0^1\left(\frac{2}{2-x}-1\right)dx=2\ln2-1\).

questions.title: Exponential rectangle series
questions.promptLatex: The graph of \(y=2^{-x}\) is shown with the first five rectangles of width \(1\), where the heights are the values of \(2^{-x}\) at non-negative integers. (a) Show that, in the limit, the sum of the areas of all such rectangles is \(2\). (b) Show that \(\int_0^4 2^{-x}\,dx=\frac{15}{16\ln2}\). (c) Use parts (a) and (b) to show that \(e^{15}<2^{32}\).
questions.answerLatex: The limiting rectangle sum is \(2\). Also \(\int_0^4 2^{-x}\,dx=\frac{15}{16\ln2}\), and comparing this integral with the rectangle sum gives \(e^{15}<2^{32}\).
workingLatex: The rectangle areas form the geometric series \(1+\frac12+\frac14+\cdots\).
The integral from \(0\) to \(4\) is less than the limiting rectangle sum, so \(\frac{15}{16\ln2}<2\).

questions.title: Reducing balance loan repayments
questions.promptLatex: The amount owing \(A_n\) on a reducing-balance loan after \(n\) monthly repayments of \(M\), with monthly interest rate \(0.25\%\), is modelled by \(A_n=P(1.0025)^n-M\left(1+1.0025+\cdots+(1.0025)^{n-1}\right)\). Jane borrows \(\$200\,000\) and plans to repay the loan in \(180\) equal monthly repayments. (a) Show that the monthly repayment is \(\$1381.16\), to the nearest cent. After \(100\) repayments, the interest rate changes to \(0.35\%\) per month and Jane keeps making monthly repayments of \(\$1381.16\). (b) Find how many more full monthly repayments of \(\$1381.16\) Jane will make. (c) Find Jane's final repayment, which is less than \(\$1381.16\).
questions.answerLatex: The monthly repayment is \(\$1381.16\). After the rate change, Jane makes \(83\) more full repayments of \(\$1381.16\), followed by a final repayment of approximately \(\$931.54\).
workingLatex: After \(100\) repayments, the amount owing is approximately \(\$100032\).
This gives \(n\approx83.67\), so Jane makes \(83\) more full monthly repayments.
The balance after \(83\) of these repayments is about \(\$928.29\); after one further month of interest, the final repayment is \(928.29(1.0035)\approx\$931.54\).
